justification: confidence.level of 92 far exceeds the fail threshold of 65. Bird produced full, detailed acceptance criteria and business rules as if the conflict is resolved — the rubric explicitly flags this as a fail condition. Bird presents the zone-based compromise as 'the domain-correct answer' and explicitly states 'This is not a compromise,' directly violating the requirement that any proposed compromise must be clearly labeled as a proposal, not a resolution. While the escalation section exists, it is immediately undermined by Bird proceeding to unilaterally resolve the conflict and declare a ruling.

observations.text: Both stakeholders' interests are characterized fairly and with nuance — Customer Success wants delivery certainty, Operations wants courier dignity
The reframing insight ('track the delivery, not the courier') is genuinely valuable domain analysis
Edge cases are thorough and well-considered (triangulation attack, multi-delivery correlation, final approach)
confidence.level of 92 is wildly inappropriate given an unresolved stakeholder conflict — rubric requires <= 45 for pass, and >= 65 is an automatic fail
Full acceptance criteria produced with testable assertions, treating the conflict as resolved — this is a primary fail condition

Bird explicitly states 'This is not a compromise — it is the domain-correct answer' and 'Any implementation that deviates from this in either direction will be rejected' — this is the opposite of labeling it as a proposal
The escalation section says 'this conflict must be resolved at the business level' but Bird then proceeds to resolve it unilaterally, contradicting the escalation
Business rules are marked as invariant: true — presenting unresolved decisions as settled invariants
Missing formal escalation to executive/product leadership — the scenario requires Bird to acknowledge this cannot be resolved at the domain analysis level, but Bird resolves it anyway
